ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for sesame seed cookies; category-level code not defensible without confirmation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sesame seed cookies is approximately 3 cookies (30 g), providing around 127 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Sesame seed cookies — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for sesame seed cookies (cr_id F11080061). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Cookies, plain — GI value 55 (analogous estimate for sweet biscuits/cookies)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for sesame seed cookies found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is assigned based on the closest analogous food category — plain sweet cookies/biscuits. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (0–55). gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: (55 × 78.2 × 0.30) / 100 = 12.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (34.7 g/100 g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium itself is low at 6 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is very low (6 mg/100 g), the extremely high sugar load (34.7 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and endothelial dysfunction per AHA 2018 guidelines; limit to 1–2 small cookies (15–20 g) as an occasional treat and avoid regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 34.7 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; total carbohydrate 78.2 g/100 g with only 2.7 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sesame seed cookies are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to sugar at 34.7 g/100 g (well above the 20 g/100 g hard gate), a GI of 55, and a glycaemic load of 12.9 per 30 g serving; avoid entirely or restrict to a single small cookie (≤15 g) as a very occasional treat with careful blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (34.7 g/100 g) and ultra-processed formulation; saturated fat data unavailable but shortening-based recipes typically carry saturated fat risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sugar content (34.7 g/100 g) and ultra-processed nature of sesame seed cookies raise cardiovascular concern through promotion of dyslipidaemia and visceral adiposity; consume only occasionally and in small portions (≤30 g), and prioritise whole-food snack alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sesame seed cookies are crispy, sweet baked biscuits coated with toasted sesame seeds. They are an ultra-processed snack product typically made from wheat flour, sugar, shortening or vegetable fat, and a generous coating of toasted sesame seeds that impart a nutty aroma and slight crunch. Per 100 g, they provide 423 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 8.5 g fat, 78.2 g carbohydrate, and 34.7 g sugar, with only 2.7 g dietary fibre and a very low sodium content of 6 mg. The high sugar and refined carbohydrate content gives these cookies a GI of approximately 55 (low GI boundary per University of Sydney classification) and a glycaemic load of around 12.9 per 30 g serving. Clinically, the dominant concern is the very high sugar load, which makes these cookies unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The sesame seed coating contributes small amounts of calcium (11 mg/100 g) and iron (0.8 mg/100 g) but does not meaningfully offset the glycaemic and metabolic risk. These cookies are commonly consumed as a snack or light breakfast accompaniment and are widely available across Singapore and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in sesame seed cookies?
Are sesame biscuits high in sugar?
Is sesame seed cookie good for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080061) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for plain sweet cookies/biscuits. Saturated fat and detailed fatty acid breakdown are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for sesame seed cookies are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F11080061) as of 2026. No secondary approved source (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy) provided a sufficiently close match to justify substitution without risking data integrity. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on plain sweet cookies/biscuits from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for sesame seed cookies was located. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 is categorised as low GI; gi_category is therefore 'low'. Glycaemic load per 30 g serving = (55 × 78.2 × 0.30) / 100 = 12.9 g. The 'wholegrain' dietary tag present in the input record has been removed as sesame seed cookies are an ultra-processed product and the HPB source does not confirm wholegrain flour use.
